plan: Fuse multiple bitonic sort stages into a single kernel using shared memory. For each step, when all remaining stages of that step fit within a shared memory tile (i.e., the sequence length <= 2*BLOCK_SIZE), load a tile of elements into shared memory, perform all sub-stages entirely in shared memory with __syncthreads() between stages, then write back once. This eliminates repeated global memory round-trips for the inner stages and amortizes kernel launch overhead. Implementation: in ParallelBitonicSort, launch a single fused kernel for all stages within a step where seq_len <= 2*blockDim.x (these are the 'local' stages), processing a tile of 2*BLOCK_SIZE elements per block with dynamic shared memory. For stages with seq_len > 2*BLOCK_SIZE, keep the existing per-stage global kernel. Use __syncthreads() between each sub-stage within the fused kernel. The fused local kernel eliminates O(step) kernel launches per step and replaces O(step) global memory round-trips per tile with a single load/store pair. Target bottleneck: 58.8% memory stall and 50% load efficiency from repeated global accesses to the same data. Risk: shared memory size limit (48KB default on sm_89 = 12K ints per block); use BLOCK_SIZE=512 or 1024 with dynamic smem allocation. Correctness risk: must ensure __syncthreads() between every compare-swap stage within the fused kernel.

Eliminate per-stage kernel launches by merging all 300 kernel dispatches into one persistent kernel that processes the entire sort. Use a grid-stride loop with __device__ grid synchronization via cooperative groups (cudaLaunchCooperativeKernel) to replace the host-side step/stage loop. All n*(n+1)/2 stages execute inside one kernel launch; global grid sync (grid.sync()) replaces the implicit synchronization from separate kernel launches. This removes all 300 cudaLaunchKernel overheads and eliminates the PCIe/driver round-trip latency per stage. Implementation: replace the host for-loop with a single cudaLaunchCooperativeKernel call; inside the kernel, loop over step and stage, compute seq_len and two_power as before, perform the swap, then call grid.sync(). BLOCK_SIZE and grid size must stay fixed. Target bottleneck: kernel launch overhead (300 launches * ~5us each = ~1.5ms potential savings) and memory stall from no L2 reuse between launches. Risk: cooperative launch requires all blocks to fit simultaneously (sm_89 supports this for 16M/256=65536 blocks which may exceed CTA concurrency limit); must check cudaOccupancyMaxActiveBlocksPerMultiprocessor and possibly reduce grid size with a stride loop. Correctness risk: grid.sync() must be called by all threads unconditionally.

Increase ILP and reduce memory latency by having each thread process multiple element pairs per kernel launch using a stride pattern. Instead of one thread handling one compare-swap pair, assign each thread 4 or 8 pairs (vectorized loop), improving arithmetic intensity and better hiding memory latency through instruction-level parallelism. Implementation: change the kernel so each thread computes its base index as i = (blockDim.x * blockIdx.x + threadIdx.x) * ITEMS_PER_THREAD, then loops ITEMS_PER_THREAD times over consecutive pairs within the same sequence. Adjust grid launch to size/(BLOCK_SIZE*ITEMS_PER_THREAD). Use __ldg() or int4 loads where pairs are contiguous to achieve 128-byte transactions. Target bottleneck: 4.6% warp issue rate (kernel too short per warp, not enough work to hide latency) and 50% load efficiency. This extends work per thread so the pipeline stays busy longer. Risk: must ensure all ITEMS_PER_THREAD pairs belong to the same sequence and direction; boundary checks needed. If pairs straddle sequence boundaries, the direction calculation per pair must be recomputed carefully.

Improve global memory coalescing and load efficiency (currently 50%) by rewriting the index computation so that threads within a warp always access consecutive memory locations. The current kernel computes swapped_ele = i + h_len, which for large seq_len causes a thread accessing index i to pair with i+h_len that is far away and in a different cache line. Restructure the launch so that for each stage, threads are assigned to work on the low-half indices in a coalesced fashion: thread k handles element k and element k XOR (seq_len/2), which for power-of-two seq_len is equivalent but allows the grid to be partitioned so consecutive threads always load consecutive addresses in the low half. Concretely, rewrite the thread index as: int half_idx = blockDim.x * blockIdx.x + threadIdx.x; int block_in_seq = half_idx / h_len; int pos_in_half = half_idx % h_len; int i = block_in_seq * seq_len + pos_in_half; int j = i + h_len. This keeps threads in a warp addressing i values that differ by 1 (coalesced), improving the 50% load efficiency toward 100%. Launch size/(2*BLOCK_SIZE) blocks. Target bottleneck: 50% global load efficiency causing wasted memory bandwidth. Risk: the restructuring must keep the seq_num/two_power/increasing logic correct; test with small n first.

Use __ldg() read-only cache intrinsics for the array reads and apply loop unrolling pragmas to reduce instruction overhead. Since the array is read and conditionally written, mark read accesses with __ldg(&a[i]) and __ldg(&a[swapped_ele]) to route loads through the 128-byte read-only (texture) cache path, which has separate bandwidth from L1/L2 and can improve effective cache hit rate for the access pattern. Add #pragma unroll to any inner loops and use __builtin_expect for the branch hint on the swap condition. Additionally, precompute seq_len as a power-of-two and use bitwise operations (i >> stage, i & (h_len-1)) instead of integer division to compute seq_num and h_len faster. Mark the kernel with __launch_bounds__(BLOCK_SIZE, 2) to hint the compiler to limit register use and increase occupancy beyond the current 80.2%. Target bottleneck: 15.9% wait stalls and 5.0% short-dependency stalls from instruction latency. Risk: __ldg() requires the pointer to be genuinely read-only for the duration of the kernel; since a[] is both read and written within the same kernel, using __ldg() may produce incorrect results if a thread reads a value already written by another thread in the same launch. Only apply __ldg() if analysis confirms no intra-kernel RAW hazards (in bitonic sort each kernel launch has no such hazards since each element pair is touched by exactly one thread). Correctness is safe per-launch.
